system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:24 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

    16|
    17|# Health Insurance Lien Management
    18|
    19|> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The health insurance lien lifecycle:
    24|
    25|1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
    26|2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
    27|3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
    28|4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
    29|5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
    30|6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
    31|7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
    36|
    37|## Agent Guidance
    38|
    39|1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
    40|2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
    41|3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
    42|4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
    43|5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
    44|6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
    45|7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially
    46|
    47|## Pitfalls
    48|
    49|- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
    50|- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
    51|- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
    52|- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
    53|- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed
    54|
    55|## Related
    56|
    57|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
    58|- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
    59|- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment
    60|", "total_lines": 59, "file_size": 3581, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Medical Records and Bills Collection
     3|slug: medical-records-collection
     4|phase: [phase_2_treatment, phase_3_demand]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [records, bills, medical, providers, hipaa, fax, email]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 3
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - pip-claims-management
    13|  - demand-timing-strategy
    14|  - provider-communication-patterns
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    18|
    19|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    24|
    25|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    26|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    27|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    28|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    29|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    30|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    31|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    36|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    37|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    38|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    39|
    40|## Agent Guidance
    41|
    42|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    43|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    44|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    45|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    46|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    47|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    48|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    49|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    50|
    51|## Pitfalls
    52|
    53|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    54|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    55|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    56|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    57|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    58|
    59|## Related
    60|
    61|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    62|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    63|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    64|", "total_lines": 63, "file_size": 3808, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: PIP Claims Management
     3|slug: pip-claims-management
     4|phase: [phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: financial
     6|tags: [pip, insurance, bills, medical-payments, ledger, kentucky]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 2
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-bill-routing-workflow
    13|  - pip-ledger-requests
    14|  - treatment-gap-defense
    15|---
    16|
    17|# PIP Claims Management
    18|
    19|> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|PIP management follows this lifecycle:
    24|
    25|1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
    26|2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
    27|3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
    28|4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
    29|5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid
    30|
    31|Key observations from case data:
    32|- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
    33|- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
    34|- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
    35|- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
    36|- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)
    37|
    38|## Evidence
    39|
    40|- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
    41|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
    42|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
    43|- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.
    44|
    45|## Agent Guidance
    46|
    47|1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
    48|2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
    49|3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
    50|4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
    51|5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
    52|6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
    53|7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
    54|8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP
    55|
    56|## Pitfalls
    57|
    58|- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
    59|- Sending bills once and not following up for months
    60|- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
    61|- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
    62|- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction
    63|
    64|## Related
    65|
    66|- [[medical-bill-routing-workflow]] — the overall process for routing medical bills
    67|- [[pip-ledger-requests]] — specifically requesting and tracking PIP ledgers
    68|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — unpaid bills and PIP delays can affect case timeline
    69|", "total_lines": 68, "file_size": 3968, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense
     3|slug: treatment-gap-defense
     4|phase: [phase_4_negotiation, phase_3_demand]
     5|category: adjuster-tactics
     6|tags: [treatment-gap, adjuster, negotiation, settlement-value, defense]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 2
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-declines-recommended-treatment
    13|  - demand-value-factors
    14|  - pip-claims-management
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense
    18|
    19|> Insurance adjusters routinely cite gaps in medical treatment as justification for lower settlement offers. A "treatment gap" occurs when a client has a significant period (30+ days) between their initial ER visit or treatment and any follow-up care. This is one of the most common defense tactics in soft-tissue PI cases.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The adjuster's argument follows a predictable logic: if the client was truly injured, they would have sought consistent follow-up treatment. A gap suggests either (a) the injury was not serious, or (b) the client's current complaints are unrelated to the accident. This defense is particularly effective when:
    24|
    25|- Client only had a single ER visit with no follow-up
    26|- Client was recommended for further treatment (chiropractic, PT) but did not follow through
    27|- The gap spans several months between the accident and any subsequent care
    28|
    29|The treatment gap defense directly reduces the perceived value of the case and is often the primary justification for low initial offers.
    30|
    31|## Evidence
    32|
    33|- In Case A (negotiation phase), the adjuster explicitly cited "the gap in treatment" as justification for the offer amount. The client had treatment at only one provider with no additional follow-up care.
    34|- In Case B (treatment phase), the client's doctor recommended chiropractic care, but the client decided against it due to difficulty coordinating treatment around work and parenting. The client's spouse acknowledged this "might be an area of risk."
    35|
    36|## Agent Guidance
    37|
    38|1. During treatment monitoring, flag any client who has not followed up with recommended care within 30 days
    39|2. If a client indicates they are stopping treatment, document the reason clearly in the activity log
    40|3. When preparing the demand, proactively address any treatment gap with an explanation (work constraints, childcare obligations, incarceration, etc.)
    41|4. Advise the attorney when a treatment gap exceeds 30 days so they can counsel the client about the impact on case value
    42|5. In negotiation, prepare counterarguments to the gap defense before the adjuster raises it
    43|
    44|## Pitfalls
    45|
    46|- Failing to document the client's reason for not pursuing recommended treatment
    47|- Not flagging the gap early enough for the attorney to address it with the client
    48|- Allowing the adjuster's gap argument to go unchallenged in negotiation
    49|- Not distinguishing between treatment gaps caused by client choice vs. external circumstances (incarceration, financial constraints, scheduling difficulties)
    50|
    51|## Related
    52|
    53|- [[client-declines-recommended-treatment]] — when clients choose not to follow through on provider recommendations
    54|- [[demand-value-factors]] — treatment continuity affects demand valuation
    55|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP benefits may still be available to cover follow-up care
    56|", "total_lines": 55, "file_size": 3222, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Case Phase Transitions
     3|slug: case-phase-transitions
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_3_demand, phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [phase-change, workflow, status, case-management, filevine]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 3
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-records-collection
    13|  - demand-timing-strategy
    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Case Phase Transitions
    18|
    19|> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Observed phase transitions across cases:
    24|
    25|### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
    26|- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
    27|- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
    28|- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
    29|- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact
    30|
    31|### File Setup -> Treatment
    32|- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
    33|- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
    34|- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
    35|- **Timeline**: Days after file setup
    36|
    37|### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
    38|- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
    39|- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
    40|- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
    41|- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)
    42|
    43|### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
    44|- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
    45|- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
    46|- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation
    47|
    48|## Evidence
    49|
    50|- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
    51|- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
    52|- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.
    53|
    54|## Agent Guidance
    55|
    56|1. Track phase change prerequisites as a checklist
    57|2. Do not advance a phase until all prerequisites are met
    58|3. Log every phase change with the person who authorized it and the date
    59|4. When an attorney asks about a case, report the current phase and what's blocking the next transition
    60|5. For transfer cases, evaluate which phases were already completed by the prior firm
    61|6. The attorney should approve all phase transitions from Demand in Progress onward
    62|
    63|## Pitfalls
    64|
    65|- Advancing to Negotiation without a demand in the file (observed in Case A)
    66|- Not confirming treatment completion with the client before transitioning to Demand
    67|- Letting cases sit in Treatment for months without checking on client status
    68|- Not logging the reason for the phase change
    69|
    70|## Related
    71|
    72|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records completion is a prerequisite for Demand
    73|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — timing of the phase transition to Demand matters
    74|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — certain transitions require attorney review
    75|", "total_lines": 74, "file_size": 3713, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Client Expectations Management
     3|slug: client-expectations-management
     4|phase: [phase_2_treatment, phase_3_demand, phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: communication
     6|tags: [client-communication, expectations, timeline, collections, updates]
     7|confidence: low

     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-communication-through-family
    13|  - medical-bill-collections-anxiety
    14|  - case-phase-transitions
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Expectations Management
    18|
    19|> PI clients (and their family members) frequently become anxious about case timelines, especially when medical bills go to collections. Proactively managing expectations about the process, timeline, and their role prevents client dissatisfaction and premature settlement pressure.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Client/family anxiety follows a predictable escalation:
    24|
    25|1. **Early Phase (0-3 months)**: Client is patient, providing information as requested
    26|2. **Mid Phase (3-6 months)**: Client begins asking "Is there anything we need to be doing?" and "Are we close to resolving this?"
    27|3. **Late Phase (6+ months)**: Client receives collection notices and expresses urgency — "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away"
    28|4. **Critical Point**: Client may pressure for quick settlement at any amount to end the process
    29|
    30|The firm's response pattern is typically reactive: responding to inquiries with brief status updates. Proactive communication is rare in the observed data.
    31|
    32|## Evidence
    33|
    34|- In Case B, the client's husband followed up approximately monthly with increasing urgency. His communications escalated from "Just checking in" (August) to "Do you feel like we are close?" (November) to "We received a letter from a debt collection agency... We want this to go away" (November) to "When can we reach a resolution? We really want to put this to bed" (December).
    35|- In Case A, the client's mother followed up asking "he was asking if I have heard anything about his case" after months of apparent inactivity.
    36|
    37|## Agent Guidance
    38|
    39|1. Set expectations at intake: explain the typical case timeline (6-18 months for most PI cases)
    40|2. Send proactive status updates at least monthly, even if the update is "waiting for records"
    41|3. When bills go to collections, explain immediately: "We have submitted this to PIP for processing. It may take up to 30 days. The collection activity will be resolved through the settlement."
    42|4. When a client asks "are we close?", give a specific answer: "We need X, Y, and Z before we can send the demand. I expect that to take approximately N weeks."
    43|5. Never leave a client inquiry unanswered for more than 24 hours
    44|6. Document client expectations and concerns in the activity log
    45|
    46|## Pitfalls
    47|
    48|- Being reactive instead of proactive with client communication
    49|- Giving vague responses like "Not at this time" without explaining what the timeline looks like
    50|- Ignoring the emotional impact of collection notices on clients
    51|- Allowing months to pass without any client contact
    52|- Not explaining the process steps that remain before resolution
    53|
    54|## Related
    55|
    56|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — many of these interactions happen through family members
    57|- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — collections are the primary driver of client urgency
    58|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — understanding the phase helps set realistic expectations
    59|", "total_lines": 58, "file_size": 3349, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Attorney Review Triggers
     3|slug: attorney-review-triggers
     4|phase: [phase_3_demand, phase_4_negotiation, phase_6_lien_resolution]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [attorney, review, escalation, decision-point, approval]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 3
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - case-phase-transitions
    13|  - settlement-offer-evaluation
    14|  - lien-management-health-insurance
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Attorney Review Triggers
    18|
    19|> Certain events in a PI case require the attorney's direct involvement and approval. These are decision points where paralegal-level handling is insufficient and attorney judgment is needed. Identifying and routing these triggers efficiently prevents bottlenecks and ensures quality control.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Attorney review is triggered by:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Case Evaluation**: When a transfer case file arrives, the attorney reviews the prior firm's work, demand status, and financial picture before directing next steps
    26|2. **Stale Case Flag**: When the attorney notices no activity on a case for an extended period (2+ months), they direct staff to check in with the client
    27|3. **Lien Disputes**: When unrelated charges appear on a health insurance lien, the attorney directs which items to dispute
    28|4. **Settlement Authority**: Client gives authority to "negotiate to LBF" (Last Best and Final) — the attorney must approve the negotiation range
    29|5. **Case Complexity Flags**: Pre-settlement funding obligations, minimal treatment, incarcerated clients — these require attorney guidance on strategy
    30|6. **Demand Approval**: Before a demand is sent, the attorney reviews and approves
    31|7. **File Completion Escalation**: When records collection stalls, the attorney escalates to ensure action
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case A, the attorney reviewed the transfer case and identified key issues: prior demand already sent, treatment gap, pre-settlement funding, and minimal treatment. He then directed staff on next steps.
    36|- In Case B, the attorney flagged the case noting "no activity since the middle of August" and directed a check-in with the client. Later, the attorney changed the phase from Treatment to Demand in Progress.
    37|- In Case C, the attorney identified that unrelated cardiac treatment was included in the Anthem lien and directed its removal. He also escalated when records requests had "no action" for a month.
    38|
    39|## Agent Guidance
    40|
    41|1. Route the following to the attorney immediately:
    42|   - New transfer case files received (with summary memo)
    43|   - Settlement offers received
    44|   - Lien itemizations with potential disputes
    45|   - Client requests for case status update (if case has been inactive 30+ days)
    46|   - Bills going to collections
    47|   - Pre-settlement funding documentation
    48|2. When routing to the attorney, include a brief summary and recommended action
    49|3. Track attorney-pending items and follow up if no response within 48 hours
    50|4. Document the attorney's direction in the activity log verbatim
    51|
    52|## Pitfalls
    53|
    54|- Not routing complex issues to the attorney (trying to handle them at paralegal level)
    55|- Routing too many routine items to the attorney (creating bottlenecks)
    56|- Not including sufficient context when routing (attorney has to dig through the file)
    57|- Not following up on items pending attorney review
    58|- Not documenting the attorney's direction
    59|
    60|## Related
    61|
    62|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — phase changes from Demand onward require attorney approval
    63|- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — offers always require attorney evaluation
    64|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — lien disputes require attorney direction
    65|", "total_lines": 64, "file_size": 3554, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Pre-Settlement Funding Obligations
    18|
    19|> Some PI clients obtain "pre-settlement funding" (lawsuit loans) from third-party lenders while their case is pending. These loans create obligations that must be satisfied from the settlement proceeds, reducing the client's net recovery. The firm must understand its obligation to the funder and factor the loan into settlement math.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Pre-settlement funding creates a complex financial picture at settlement:
    24|
    25|1. Client takes a loan from a funding company (e.g., "Claims Angel") during the pendency of the case
    26|2. The loan accrues interest and fees over time
    27|3. At settlement, the funding must be repaid from the client's share of proceeds
    28|4. The attorney must factor the loan payoff into the disbursement sheet
    29|
    30|Settlement math with pre-settlement funding:
    31|- Gross settlement
    32|- Less: Attorney fees
    33|- Less: Case costs
    34|- Less: Medical provider payments/liens
    35|- Less: Pre-settlement funding payoff
    36|- Equals: Client's net recovery
    37|
    38|When the settlement offer is modest, pre-settlement funding can consume a large portion of what's left, leaving the client with very little.
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case A, the client had "loans" from a company named "Claims Angel" totaling $6,537.05. With an $18K offer, after paying the loan ($6,537.05) and 50% of the medical provider bill ($5,397.50), and attorney fees, the client would receive less than $3K. The paralegal noted: "I'm not sure what obligation we have to satisfy the presettlement funding."
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. During intake, ask if the client has obtained any pre-settlement funding or lawsuit loans
    47|2. If yes, request the funding agreement and current payoff amount
    48|3. Log the funder name, original loan amount, and current payoff in the case financial records
    49|4. Factor the funding payoff into all settlement evaluations
    50|5. When presenting settlement options to the client, show the complete disbursement breakdown including the funding payoff
    51|6. Ask the attorney about the firm's legal obligation to satisfy the funding (varies by jurisdiction and agreement)
    52|7. If the settlement barely covers obligations, flag this to the attorney early
    53|
    54|## Pitfalls
    55|
    56|- Not discovering the pre-settlement funding until settlement disbursement
    57|- Not understanding the firm's legal obligation to the funder
    58|- Not factoring funding into the settlement evaluation, leading to unrealistic client expectations
    59|- Assuming the payoff amount is the same as the original loan (interest and fees accrue)
    60|
    61|## Related
    62|
    63|- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — funding obligations affect whether an offer is adequate
    64|- [[demand-value-factors]] — knowing the funding obligation influences demand strategy
    65|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens and funding together determine net recovery
    66|", "total_lines": 65, "file_size": 3189, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

# VA (Veterans Affairs) Lien Management

> When a PI client is a veteran receiving care through the VA healthcare system, the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs asserts a federal lien for the cost of accident-related treatment under the Federal Medical Care Recovery Act (42 U.S.C. § 2651). VA liens are managed by the VA Office of General Counsel (OGC) Revenue Law Group — a specialized federal legal team. This process is fundamentally different from private health insurance liens and requires distinct handling.

## Pattern

The VA lien lifecycle is significantly more complex and slower than private insurer liens:

1. **Initial Lien Request**: Firm mails an initial lien request to VA Healthcare early in file setup. This must be sent to the correct VA facility (e.g., VA Medical Center Lexington).
2. **Case Assignment**: VA OGC Revenue Law Group assigns a Paralegal Specialist who becomes the single point of contact. Cases are tracked with a GCL case number (e.g., "596-W-20231106-115744/GCL #537467").
3. **Claim Assertion**: The VA Revenue Law Group asserts a specific dollar amount for accident-related treatment (e.g., "$4,437.13 is the VA's claim in this case").
4. **Records vs. Billing Separation**: The VA separates records requests from billing requests. Records must come from the VA facility; billing is handled by OGC. Sending a records request to the billing fax line (or vice versa) causes delays.
5. **Case Closure and Reopening**: VA cases can be "closed" (with payment issued to the VA) and then need to be "reopened" if additional treatment dates are discovered. The VA case manager will request a list of treatment dates to evaluate reopening.
6. **PIP Payment of VA Lien**: PIP carriers can be directed to pay the VA lien directly. In one case, the PIP carrier issued payment to the Department of Veterans Affairs for the lien amount upon receiving a direction-to-pay with the VA billing statement.
7. **Settlement Integration**: The VA lien must be satisfied from settlement proceeds. The VA expects "full reimbursement" plus any additional new-money settlement.

### Key Differences from Private Health Insurance Liens

| Aspect | Private Health Insurance | VA Lien |
|--------|------------------------|---------|
| Legal basis | State subrogation law / ERISA | Federal Medical Care Recovery Act (42 U.S.C. § 2651) |
| Contact | Claims/subrogation department | OGC Revenue Law Group (Paralegal Specialist) |
| Tracking | Claim number | GCL case number |
| Response time | Days to weeks | Weeks to months |
| Records access | Via provider records dept | Must go through VA facility; veteran can also use MyHealtheVet portal |
| Negotiation | Percentage reductions common | Federal government — less flexibility |
| Case lifecycle | Stays open until resolved | Can be closed and must be reopened |

## Evidence

- In Case D, the initial lien request was mailed to VA Healthcare on day 1 of file setup (July 2023). It took until July 2024 — a full year — before the VA's Revenue Law Group Paralegal Specialist responded with the lien amount of $4,437.13. During that year, multiple follow-up requests were required.
- The VA case was then closed in August 2024 when PIP paid the VA lien. However, when additional VA treatment was discovered later, the firm had to request the case be reopened. The VA case manager asked for "a list of dates you are including" before reopening.
- The firm was unable to provide treatment dates without first obtaining the records, creating a circular dependency: the VA wanted dates to reopen; the firm needed records to identify dates. The paralegal pushed back: "We are unable to provide a list of dates related without the requested records to review."
- The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs for $4,437.13 upon receiving direction to pay along with the VA billing statement.
- The attorney noted the case was fundamentally complicated by VA involvement: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
- The BI adjuster (who was himself an Air Force veteran) recognized the VA lien dynamics in his voicemail, noting the VA "wants their money back" and structured his new-money offer around the VA balance.

## Agent Guidance

1. At intake, ask if the client receives any medical care through the VA. If yes, flag the case as "VA lien likely."
2. Send the initial lien request to VA Healthcare by mail during file setup — do not delay.
3. When the VA OGC Revenue Law Group assigns a case manager, record their name, phone, email, and the GCL case number in the case file.
4. All future correspondence must reference the GCL case number in the subject line.
5. Do NOT use veteran PII in unencrypted email to VA — reference GCL number only.
6. Do NOT send billing requests to the new-case intake fax line — that is for new cases only. Use the assigned case manager's direct contact.
7. Track VA records and VA billing as separate requests with separate follow-up timelines.
8. Set follow-up reminders at 30-day intervals — VA response times are measured in months, not days.
9. When PIP is available, consider directing PIP to pay the VA lien directly as a way to resolve the lien early.
10. If the VA case is closed and additional treatment is discovered, immediately contact the case manager with the list of new treatment dates to request reopening.
11. Advise the veteran client to access their own records via MyHealtheVet (https://www.myhealth.va.gov/) as a parallel path to obtain records faster.
12. When sorting VA records, have a paralegal review for "relatedness" to the accident — VA records will include all treatment, not just accident-related care.

## Pitfalls

- Expecting VA lien resolution to move at private-insurer speed (it does not — plan for months)
- Sending records requests to the wrong VA fax number (billing fax vs. records fax vs. intake fax)
- Not recording the GCL case number — all correspondence requires it
- Including veteran PII in unencrypted emails to VA
- Assuming the VA lien amount is final without checking for additional treatment dates
- Not realizing the VA case can be "closed" and then need reopening
- Failing to involve the client in records gathering — veterans can access their own records through the VA portal
- Not sorting VA records for accident-relatedness before using them in the demand (VA records include all care, not just MVA-related)
- Circular dependency trap: VA wants treatment dates to reopen case; firm needs records to identify dates. Break this cycle by having the client provide dates or use MyHealtheVet.

## Related

- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — contrast with private health insurance lien process
- [[medical-records-collection]] — VA records collection requires distinct approach
- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — detailed guide to government entity records processes
- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP can be used to pay VA liens directly
- [[multi-carrier-negotiation]] — VA lien adds a third party to the BI/PIP coordination
"}

# Government Entity Medical Records Collection

> Collecting medical records from government healthcare providers (VA Medical Centers, military treatment facilities, TRICARE) is fundamentally slower and more procedurally complex than collecting from private providers. Government entities have separate fax lines for records vs. billing, specialized departments (VA OGC Revenue Law Group), and response times measured in months rather than days. Cases involving government providers require distinct tracking and escalation strategies.

## Pattern

Government entity records collection differs from private providers in several critical ways:

1. **Multiple Contact Points**: Unlike private providers where one records department handles everything, government entities split responsibilities:
   - Records requests go to the VA facility's Release of Information (ROI) department
   - Billing requests go to either the facility billing manager or the VA OGC Revenue Law Group (if a lien case)
   - Lien correspondence goes to the assigned OGC Paralegal Specialist
   - New case intake has a separate fax line from existing cases

2. **Extended Timeline**: In observed cases, the records collection from the VA took 12-18 months from initial request to receipt:
   - Initial lien request mailed: Month 0
   - Records request mailed: Month 4
   - Follow-up records/bills request: Month 6
   - VA OGC responds with lien amount: Month 12
   - Updated records request faxed: Month 13
   - Records finally received: Month 17+

3. **Incomplete Records**: VA records often arrive incomplete. The client may have ongoing treatment (monthly visits, injections, physical therapy programs like IPRP) that does not appear in the records received. Client self-report may reveal treatment not captured in the initial records release.

4. **Client as Records Source**: Veterans can access and download their own health records through MyHealtheVet (https://www.myhealth.va.gov/). This is often faster than the formal request process and can serve as a bridge while waiting for official records.

5. **Relatedness Sorting Required**: VA records include ALL treatment, not just accident-related care. A paralegal must sort VA records for relatedness before they can be used in demand preparation.

## Evidence

- In Case D, the initial lien request to VA Healthcare was mailed in July 2023. A formal records/bills request was sent in November 2023, then again in December 2023. The VA OGC Revenue Law Group did not respond with the lien amount until July 2024. Updated records were requested in July 2024. VA records were still arriving in late 2024/early 2025.
- The attorney directed staff to "sort the VA records for relatedness" in November 2024, indicating the records included a mix of accident-related and unrelated treatment.
- In October 2025, the attorney noted "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA" — the client reported multiple MRIs, physical therapy through the VA's IPRP program, injections, and ongoing treatment that was not in the file. The firm had to "hand hold the VA to get all this stuff."
- The firm sent the client all VA records on file and asked the client to "review and provide me with a list of what is missing along with any other providers which we may need to obtain records from."
- A records request faxed to the wrong VA fax line (the new-case intake line) was redirected by the OGC case manager, who admonished: "This case is not a new case... the intake fax line is for new cases only."

## Agent Guidance

1. At file setup, identify whether any providers are government entities (VA, military, TRICARE).
2. If VA is involved, send the initial lien notification AND records request as two separate communications on day 1.
3. Maintain a separate tracking timeline for government entities — use 30-day follow-up intervals instead of 14-day.
4. Record all VA-specific reference numbers: GCL case number, facility case number, case manager name/phone/email.
5. Advise the client to log into MyHealtheVet and download their records as a parallel path.
6. When VA records arrive, have a dedicated paralegal sort them for accident-relatedness before adding to the demand file.
7. Ask the client to provide a complete list of all VA treatment since the accident — do not rely solely on the records you receive.
8. Confirm which VA fax number is correct for your specific request type before faxing.
9. When demand preparation is blocked by incomplete VA records, consider sending a demand with available records and supplementing later, rather than waiting indefinitely.
10. Expect that you will need at least 2-3 follow-up requests before receiving complete VA records.

## Pitfalls

- Using the same follow-up cadence for VA as for private providers (VA requires much longer timelines)
- Assuming the VA records received are complete (they are almost certainly not)
- Not involving the client in identifying missing treatment
- Faxing to the wrong VA department (billing vs. records vs. intake)
- Not sorting records for relatedness before use in demand
- Letting VA records delays block the entire case indefinitely — consider sending demand with what you have
- Not asking the client to use MyHealtheVet as a parallel records source

## Related

- [[medical-records-collection]] — standard records collection process (contrast with government entities)
- [[va-lien-management]] — the VA lien process that runs parallel to records collection
- [[records-completion-gates-demand]] — how VA records delays cascade into demand timeline delays
"}

# Adjuster Reassignment Patterns

> Insurance carriers frequently reassign adjusters during the life of a PI claim, sometimes multiple times. Each reassignment resets the negotiation relationship and often causes delays as the new adjuster reviews the file from scratch. In long-running cases, the firm may interact with 3-5 different adjusters on a single claim. Understanding this pattern helps manage expectations and maintain negotiation momentum.

## Pattern

Adjuster reassignment follows observable patterns:

1. **Initial Adjuster**: Handles the claim from opening through liability investigation. May make the first offer.
2. **Mid-Case Reassignment**: Common at 6-12 months. May coincide with carrier restructuring or adjuster caseload rebalancing.
3. **Stale File Reassignment**: When a case has had no movement for extended periods, carriers may reassign to a "closer" adjuster who proactively reaches out to resolve.
4. **New Adjuster Introduction**: New adjusters typically introduce themselves, note they've been "reassigned" to the file, and request updated documentation.
5. **Fresh Eyes Effect**: A new adjuster sometimes brings movement — they re-evaluate the file without the baggage of prior negotiations and may make a new offer that differs from the prior adjuster's position.

### Impact on Negotiations

- Each reassignment adds 2-4 weeks of delay as the new adjuster reviews the file
- New adjusters may not honor informal commitments made by prior adjusters
- A new adjuster may request documentation already provided to the prior adjuster
- However, reassignment can be an opportunity: the new adjuster may be more willing to negotiate than a predecessor who was entrenched in a position

## Evidence

- In Case D, the BI claim went through at least 3 adjusters over 2+ years:
  - Adjuster 1 (initial): Handled the claim during the liability investigation phase, while coverage was still "under review"
  - Adjuster 2: Made the initial offer. After receiving updated records, the claim was "reassigned to a different adjuster" with the hope it would "cause some movement."
  - Adjuster 3: Self-identified as "the new adjuster on this file." Left multiple voicemails proactively reaching out, noting "there really has not been any movement" and "I was hoping to... try to follow up." This adjuster made multiple attempts to reach the attorney over several weeks before connecting.
  - Adjuster 4: Subsequently reassigned again; the new adjuster reviewed additional treatment records and made the carrier's "top offer."
- The Adjuster 3 reassignment was particularly notable: this adjuster was proactive, called multiple times, left detailed voicemails expressing willingness to negotiate, and explicitly said "I think that if we could have a phone conversation about this, we could get it resolved."
- After submitting updated records, the carrier reassigned to Adjuster 4, who raised the offer but also introduced a new defense (attributing treatment to an unrelated slip-and-fall incident).

## Agent Guidance

1. When a new adjuster introduces themselves, log their name, direct phone, extension, and email immediately.
2. Proactively send the new adjuster a summary of the case status and any prior correspondence/offers.
3. Treat a new adjuster as an opportunity — respond promptly to their outreach.
4. If a new adjuster calls and leaves a voicemail, return the call within 24 hours (they are motivated to work the file).
5. Keep a chronological log of all adjusters who have handled the claim.
6. When the new adjuster requests documentation, send it promptly rather than assuming they have the prior adjuster's files.
7. Alert the attorney when an adjuster change occurs, as this may be a strategic opportunity to re-engage.

## Pitfalls

- Ignoring a new adjuster's outreach (especially when they are proactively trying to settle)
- Assuming the new adjuster has all documentation from the prior adjuster
- Not updating contact information when a reassignment occurs
- Missing the window of opportunity when a "closer" adjuster is assigned and motivated
- Letting weeks pass without responding to a new adjuster's repeated outreach

## Related

- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — new adjusters may re-evaluate treatment gap arguments
- [[client-expectations-management]] — adjuster changes add to client frustration about delays
- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — adjusters may reference the approaching SOL as urgency to settle
"}

# Multi-Carrier Claim Coordination

> Many PI cases involve simultaneous claims with multiple insurance carriers and government entities: a BI (Bodily Injury) claim against the at-fault party's insurer, a PIP claim for medical payments, and one or more liens (health insurance, VA, Medicare/Medicaid). Coordinating these parallel claims — each with different adjusters, timelines, and processes — is one of the most complex operational challenges in PI practice. Failure to coordinate can result in double-billing disputes, delayed settlements, and reduced client recovery.

## Pattern

A complex multi-carrier case involves these parallel streams:

### Stream 1: BI (Bodily Injury) Claim
- Against the at-fault party's insurer
- Dependent on completed records for demand preparation
- Negotiation involves offers, counter-offers, and the final settlement
- The BI settlement is the primary source of client recovery

### Stream 2: PIP (Personal Injury Protection) Claim
- Through the client's own insurer (in PIP states like KY)
- Bills submitted with "direction to pay" as they come in
- PIP can pay providers directly, including paying VA liens
- PIP payments reduce the amount that must come from the BI settlement
- PIP ledger needed before BI demand is sent

### Stream 3: Government/Health Insurance Liens
- VA liens, Medicare conditional payments, health insurance subrogation
- Must be identified, quantified, and factored into settlement math
- Payment may come from PIP (before settlement) or from settlement proceeds
- Federal liens (VA, Medicare) have different legal frameworks than private liens

### Coordination Points

The critical coordination moments are:

1. **File Setup**: Identify ALL insurance streams and liens. Set up separate tracking for each.
2. **Bill Routing**: Each medical bill must be routed to the correct payer:
   - VA treatment bills → VA lien (may be paid by PIP)
   - Private provider bills → PIP first, then any remainder to health insurance
   - Bills not covered by PIP/health insurance → BI demand specials
3. **PIP Directing to Pay VA Lien**: PIP carriers can be directed to pay VA liens directly, effectively using the client's PIP benefits to satisfy the government lien. This reduces the VA lien's impact on the BI settlement.
4. **Demand Preparation**: Before sending the BI demand, you need:
   - Updated PIP ledger showing all payments
   - Current VA lien amount (adjusted for any PIP payments)
   - All medical specials from all providers
5. **Settlement Math**: The BI settlement must account for remaining unpaid liens, attorney fees, and case costs. Multi-carrier cases have more deductions, often leaving less for the client.

### The "New Money" vs. "Total Value" Distinction

In multi-carrier cases, the BI adjuster may frame offers as "new money" — meaning money on top of what PIP and other sources have already paid. This distinction is important:
- Total medical specials: Sum of all treatment costs
- Already paid by PIP: Reduces what the BI settlement needs to cover
- VA lien amount: Must be satisfied (may already be paid by PIP)
- "New money" from BI: What the BI carrier pays in addition to PIP payments
- Client's net: New money minus attorney fees, costs, and any remaining liens

## Evidence

- In Case D, the case involved three carriers and a government lien simultaneously:
  - BI claim with a small regional carrier (at-fault party's insurer)
  - PIP claim with a national carrier (client's own insurer)
  - VA lien managed by VA OGC Revenue Law Group ($4,437.13)
  - The PIP carrier was directed to pay the VA lien directly, which it did
  - The BI adjuster framed his offer as "new money" of $4,200 on top of the VA payment, saying "the VA wants their money back" and noting the PIP had already handled the VA balance
  - Later adjusters increased the offer, but also introduced defenses (attributing treatment to an unrelated incident)
  - The final settlement math required tracking: PIP payments, VA lien satisfaction, chiro bills (disputed for relatedness), PT bills, and the BI offer
- The PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was for a condition that "gotten worse a month prior for no apparent reason" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness."

## Agent Guidance

1. At file setup, create a coordination matrix listing every carrier, claim number, adjuster, and lien:
   | Stream | Carrier | Claim # | Adjuster | Status |
   |--------|---------|---------|----------|--------|
   | BI | [Carrier] | [#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
   | PIP | [Carrier] | [#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
   | VA Lien | VA OGC | [GCL#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
2. Track bill routing: for each medical bill, record which carrier(s) it was submitted to and the payment status.
3. When a VA lien is identified and PIP is available, discuss with the attorney whether to direct PIP to pay the VA lien.
4. Before preparing the BI demand, ensure you have current ledgers/amounts from ALL streams.
5. When presenting settlement offers to the attorney, show the complete multi-carrier picture:
   - BI offer (new money)
   - PIP payments already made
   - VA/lien status (satisfied by PIP or outstanding)
   - Net to client after all deductions
6. When a PIP adjuster disputes relatedness of treatment, obtain additional documentation from the provider and resubmit.
7. Monitor SOL separately — the BI statute may expire while you are still coordinating VA records/liens.

## Pitfalls

- Not identifying all insurance streams at intake
- Losing track of which bills have been submitted to which carrier
- Not understanding the "new money" distinction when evaluating BI offers
- Letting VA lien delays hold up the entire BI negotiation
- Not using PIP to pay VA liens when PIP benefits are available
- Assuming the PIP adjuster will accept all bills as accident-related (they audit for relatedness too)
- Not having complete ledgers from all carriers before preparing the BI demand

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP claim management as one stream in multi-carrier coordination
- [[va-lien-management]] — VA lien as a unique stream requiring distinct handling
- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — health insurance lien as another stream
- [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] — additional deduction from settlement that compounds multi-carrier complexity
"}
